**David Alan LaCerte** (born November 6, 1979) is currently a member of the [Federal Energy Regulatory Commission](/wiki/Federal_Energy_Regulatory_Commission "Federal Energy Regulatory Commission"). He was previously the White House Liaison and Senior Advisor to the Director for U.S. Office of Personnel Management (OPM).[[1]](#cite_note-1) LaCerte previously served as an appointee of [President Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump") as a senior advisor at OPM from 2020-2021.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

Prior to LaCerte's second stint at OPM, he served as Special Counsel at Baker Botts.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

In January 2021, he was appointed as a Senior Advisor and Executive Counsel at the [U.S. Chemical Safety and Hazard Investigation Board](/wiki/U.S._Chemical_Safety_and_Hazard_Investigation_Board "U.S. Chemical Safety and Hazard Investigation Board") and served as Acting Managing Director.

LaCerte was the Secretary of the [Louisiana Department of Veterans Affairs](/wiki/Louisiana_Department_of_Veterans_Affairs "Louisiana Department of Veterans Affairs") (LDVA) in the administration of Governor [Bobby Jindal](/wiki/Bobby_Jindal "Bobby Jindal").[[4]](#cite_note-4) He had been the Interim Secretary from January 2013 to September 2013.[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)

On July 17, 2025, he was nominated by [President Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump") to FERC for a term that ends June 30, 2026.[[7]](#cite_note-7) He was confirmed en bloc on a party-line vote in October, 2025.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

## Military career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=David_LaCerte&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Military career")]

LaCerte served in the [United States Marine Corps](/wiki/United_States_Marine_Corps "United States Marine Corps") [Infantry](/wiki/Infantry "Infantry") at [1st Battalion 1st Marines](/wiki/1st_Battalion,_1st_Marines "1st Battalion, 1st Marines") (1/1). Shortly after the [September 11 attacks](/wiki/September_11_attacks "September 11 attacks") in 2001, he was deployed to Afghanistan under the command of General [James Mattis](/wiki/James_Mattis "James Mattis"). LaCerte led over 100 combat patrols and missions in Afghanistan and Pakistan and served on interrogation teams for high value [Al Qaeda](/wiki/Al_Qaeda "Al Qaeda") targets.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

## Louisiana Department of Veterans Affairs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=David_LaCerte&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Louisiana Department of Veterans Affairs")]

LaCerte is a graduate of [Nicholls State University](/wiki/Nicholls_State_University "Nicholls State University") and [Paul M. Hebert Law Center](/wiki/Paul_M._Hebert_Law_Center "Paul M. Hebert Law Center"). He joined the LDVA in 2010 as Deputy Secretary and Executive Counsel under Secretary [Lane Carson](/wiki/Lane_Carson "Lane Carson"). Following the retirement of Carson, LaCerte served as the Interim Secretary of LDVA until the appointment of former US Representative [Rodney Alexander](/wiki/Rodney_Alexander "Rodney Alexander") in September 2013. LaCerte was appointed by Governor Bobby Jindal as Secretary after Alexander resigned on June 3, 2014.[[10]](#cite_note-:2-10)

Under the leadership of Secretary LaCerte, LDVA experienced a historic period of financial reforms, becoming the first state to transition completely from state appropriations in the Veteran Homes program, expanding Parish Service Offices to every parish in the state and doubling Louisiana's VA Disability Payments to over $1 billion, and building three additional state veterans' cemeteries at Fort Polk, [Slidell](/wiki/Slidell,_Louisiana "Slidell, Louisiana"), and [Rayville](/wiki/Rayville,_Louisiana "Rayville, Louisiana").[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12)

LaCerte was the first chairman of the Louisiana Military Advisory Council, tasked with reinforcing the state's military footprint in the face of potentially devastating cuts of thousands of soldiers and family members at Fort Polk. Due to the efforts of the LMAC and LaCerte as chairman, [Fort Polk](/wiki/Fort_Polk "Fort Polk") escaped major cuts which would have been catastrophic to the local and state economies.[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14)

LDVA also sponsored and passed legislation in many areas, including benefits reform, military spouse occupational license reciprocity, and some of the early Veterans Treatment Courts in the nation.[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17)

LaCerte resigned in October 2015 amid two investigations into his office, prompted by a report, released in August 2015, that turned up concerns about conditions and lack of oversight at nursing homes for veterans.[[18]](#cite_note-:0-18)[[19]](#cite_note-:1-19) The investigations by the Louisiana legislative auditor and the State Inspector General's Office found possible misspending of funds and failure to report alleged violence against a veteran.[[19]](#cite_note-:1-19)

LaCerte famously called the report "garbage" and sued the state, the legislative auditor, and the Inspector General for defamation.[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21)

## Office of Personnel Management

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=David_LaCerte&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Office of Personnel Management")]

In June 2020, LaCerte left the private practice of law as a partner at the Louisiana law firm of Sternberg Naccari and White and was appointed by the White House as a [senior advisor](/wiki/Senior_Advisor_to_the_President_of_the_United_States "Senior Advisor to the President of the United States") at the [Office of Personnel Management](/wiki/United_States_Office_of_Personnel_Management "United States Office of Personnel Management") where he served in policy and as a special counsel in the administration of the President.[[22]](#cite_note-22)
